Trump says US-Iran deal is signed and Strait of Hormuz opens Friday
articles.summary: Trump says US-Iran deal is signed and Strait of Hormuz opens Friday Trump says the agreement with Iran is "all signed" and that the Strait of Hormuz will be "completely opened" on Friday. Speaking on Monday, Trump said Iran would not obtain a nuclear weapon under the deal and added that Washington and Tehran would "hopefully" build a "good relationship". He also said oil prices were "plummeting" and that stocks were rising after the announcement.

articles.summary: US vice president says Iran could access $300bn reconstruction fund US Vice President JD Vance has suggested Iran could gain access to $300bn in reconstruction funds if it complies with the tentative peace deal with Washington. Asked by CBS News’s Ed O’Keefe whether the reported funding package was part of the agreement, Vance said it was possible if Tehran honoured its commitments. "That's the sort of thing they could have access to, funded by the Gulf coast coalition, so long as they honour their end of the obligation," Vance said. articles.title: Iranian president says deal with US could become a ‘honourable document’
articles.summary: Iranian president says deal with US could become a ‘honourable document’ Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian says the memorandum of understanding could be seen as an "honourable document" for Iran if all sides implement it properly, according to state-run Irib and the semi-official Fars news agency. Fars also cited Pezeshkian as saying that 90 percent of Supreme National Security Council members had voted in favour of the agreement. He said the details would be approved at the "appropriate time".

articles.title: Knicks Win, Giving Tortured Fans First NBA Title in 53 Years

articles.title: Warsh Caught Between Trump, Bond Market Bet on Rate Hikes
articles.summary: Just three weeks into the job, Federal Reserve Chairman Kevin Warsh is already facing an unusually high-stakes test. Inflation is roaring back at the fastest pace in three years. There’s growing dissent among the central bank’s policymakers. And investors have been dumping US Treasury bonds and piling into bets the Fed will need to start raising rates by December, defying President Trump’s call to lower them instead.
